data.title: ADX: Nearly 100 Years Of Success
data.description:
We all have strong opinions. You may not have strong opinions on every single subject, but at the end of the day, every one of us has a strong opinion about something.

You don't have to look very far on Seeking Alpha to find analysts with strong opinions about different companies or funds that are available in the market. It's no secret that some investors love to invest in funds, and other investors find them to be completely undesirable. The same can be said for analysts. Some analysts love to use funds as their primary form of portfolio construction, and other analysts decide that funds are completely outside their desired scope and focus. Part of the reason for this is that funds as registered investment companies (RIC) usually see some level of net asset value (NAV) decay over their lifetime. Because of their RIC status, they must pay out 90% of their taxable gains and taxable revenue annually. This means that any slip-up by the fund manager or over-distribution of their collected revenue can lead the fund to steadily see NAV erosion and decline.

It's easy to point out funds that are doing this, like Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC), Eagle Point Credit (ECC), and XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT). Many fund managers use the yield like a bug zapper light to draw in retail investors who are hungry for income today, only to see that the capital they put in their fund has eroded faster than the cash the fund has paid out to them. Some funds are explicitly structured to pay you a massive amount of money that is never going to be sustainable, like Cornerstone Strategic Investment Fund (CLM). Other funds will put on the facade of being able to afford their distribution, only for the truth to come out when the music stops.

What if I told you that there is a fund that is older than the most well-beloved passive market-wide ETF? What if I told you that this fund has actually outperformed that ETF in total returns over that ETF's lifetime?

Let's check it out.

Nearly 100 Years of Success

Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX) holds a unique distinction of being one of the oldest funds in the United States. ADX was founded in 1929 and survived the Great Depression, numerous wars, and varied economic conditions.

A major drawback of investing in most funds is NAV decay, so one might expect a fund that is nearly 100 years old to have suffered from it considerably. Funds with 20-year histories often trade at massively lower valuations than they saw originally. ADX bucks this trend. Its NAV has climbed 190.8% since the 90s.

I like to compare ADX to one of the oldest S&P 500 passive market-wide index funds. This would be the State Street SPDR S&P 500 Trust (SPY). As you can see, over the same time frame, it has outperformed it by nearly 300% as far as total returns. However, when we look at price change, the vast majority of its returns come from the distributions that it pays out. As an RIC, ADX is required to pay out 90% of its taxable income or returns as distributions to its shareholders, so while the fund's value has only climbed 190%, it's provided over 2980% returns.

ADX provides this substantial outperformance due to its focus on investing in some of the largest names and the highest quality names within the S&P 500. Currently, it has a nearly 8% exposure to Nvidia (NVDA) and a nearly 7% exposure to Apple (AAPL). Due to its large, focused bets on strong companies, it's enjoyed a strong outperformance versus the index itself.

ADX has a managed distribution policy. This means that the fund itself is not focused on whether it is successfully covering the distributions it pays out. It pays out a fixed percentage of its NAV every single quarter - 2% per quarter, totaling 8% annually. This means that its distributions will be varied based upon the performance of the fund, but this varied distribution ensures that the managers are not trying to game shareholders. They know exactly how much to expect based upon how the NAV of the fund is doing. If NAV continues to climb, the distribution will continue to climb. If NAV dips, the distribution will dip, helping ensure that it remains sustainable.

This change occurred in 2024. In the prior iteration of their distribution policy, ADX would pay out a token dividend every single quarter and then a massive year-end distribution at the end to meet their requirements as an RIC. So far, this change in distribution policy has not led to any sort of NAV erosion in the fund. The distribution at this point is still considered readily sustainable.

What I would caution against is viewing the variable dividend as being hiked or cut. It is simply a set percentage of NAV and therefore does not truly experience what other funds do, where they raise or cut their distribution based on management's viewpoint.

ADX gives you an easy way to have exposure to some of the larger names in the S&P 500 index that may pay a tiny yield. If you're an investor who is nearing retirement or in retirement and wants exposure to the returns of the market but wants them in a distribution of cash instead of capital gains, ADX can fill that hole. For investors who are still building up their capital base or have a longer time frame before retirement, it does not replace investing in some of these names directly or looking for greater capital gains on top of your distributions. ADX is a great way to boost the income you're receiving from the market today while still gaining exposure to the upside of the market over the long run. The outperformance of ADX versus the index helps solidify it as an excellent holding for one's portfolio over the long run. There are very few CEFs or funds in existence that can boast a nearly 100-year history with strong performance.

Conclusion

ADX is a great way for a passive market-wide ETF investor to see outsized returns, primarily providing them as cash in hand. It also readily bucks the trend of most CEFs in the fact that it has not seen steady or regular NAV decay over its lifetime.

As a dividend investor who looks for undervalued companies to invest in that are steadily growing their dividend over the long run, ADX can fill a hole that might otherwise be created by that focus. ADX can provide you with high yields with market-wide exposure while augmenting the rest of your portfolio. I don't believe in putting my entire portfolio or even a vast majority of my portfolio into funds because I believe that individually selecting high-quality companies can provide me with high-quality returns in the end. Yet I'm also not blind to the fact that as one approaches retirement, they are going to want greater sums of cash from their portfolio and not be forced to have to make the decision of when to sell holdings as often. ADX allows you to outsource that requirement. The portfolio manager manages the portfolio, the buying and the selling, and simply provides you cash as a return from it. As we've seen since the 90s, ADX has seen its NAV almost double. This means that you don't have to worry that the cash they're handing you is simply your own money being handed back to you, because the value of your holdings is climbing along with the cash that's being poured out to you.

There's no point in building a financial fortress quality retirement portfolio only to have to sell the individual bricks and stones of your portfolio to put bread on your table. There's also no point in buying holdings that are going to crumble over time and lose value because of their low quality.

Demand quality. Become a Dividend King.

data.title: Market's Impatience Is Growing My Income: Greystone Housing
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Price is what you pay, value is what you get. It's a great line for a fortune cookie, but one that is more challenging to apply to investing than you might think. The core problem is that you have to be able to recognize value when a large portion of the market doesn't.

Today, we're going to take a look at a company that has a very long track record of paying stable dividends. For decades, it paid a stable amount. In 2015, it added a more variable component to its strategy. This resulted in a dividend cut in 2020, large special distributions from 2021-2022, a higher than pre-COVID distribution in 2023-2024, and then a distribution cut in 2025 and now another cut in 2026.

The instability of the distribution hasn't won a lot of support in the market, and the company found itself trading at low valuations relative to its own history. Management has telegraphed its intention to go back to its roots, to exit the variable strategy and go back to the strategy that produced stable and predictable distributions for decades.

The market hates it and isn't willing to wait. The more they sell, the more I buy.

GHI

Greystone Housing Impact Investors LP (GHI), yielding 11.1%, is a unique partnership that invests in multi-family housing using two distinct strategies.

At Q3 2025 earnings, management telegraphed a dividend cut. We observed:

"Given the commentary, the last seems unlikely. We think something around $0.25 is probably favored, but we also wouldn't blame management if they just decide to bring the distribution back to 2020 levels. It might be better to be conservative and increase the distribution with each sale, than to risk sitting here a year from now with no properties sold and having to cut again."

Here we sit about 5 months later, and none of the JV (Joint Venture) properties have been sold, and apparently, the outlook is that none are going to be sold soon. GHI has decided to cut its distribution even more aggressively to $0.14/unit.

Arguably, it would have been better for optics if they had just slashed it enough last year, instead of choosing a level that required cash from their JV strategy. No doubt the market will be on edge for a while until GHI proves that the bottom is in. Ripping the band-aid off quickly probably would have been better than the path of multiple cuts.

There is no question that in terms of communication, management dropped the ball. But we aren't interested in great communication, we are interested in making money.

What GHI Does

GHI has two distinct businesses:

  • Their mortgage revenue bonds, where they buy bonds on a leveraged basis and profit from the interest received. This is GHI's original business that they executed for decades.
  • Their JV strategy where they develop multi-family apartments and sell them. Cash flow is minimal to negative until the property is sold. GHI started this strategy in 2015.

So, a portion of their cash flow is recurring, while the other portion is very lumpy. Here is a slide from GHI's 2022 earnings, when their properties were selling like hotcakes: Source

Note all that red, that was gains on sales of their JV properties. The gain on sale is after subtracting expenses and principal. It was a very sizable portion of their cash flow, and it was those sales that drove the distribution increases and special distributions that GHI paid through that period.

Fast forward to today, and properties aren't selling. GHI has nine properties where construction is completed, totaling 2,456 units. They have commitments to provide funding to build up three more properties with 706 combined units. If their JV partner decides not to proceed forward, the land will be sold, and GHI will get its principal back. Source

GHI has properties to sell, but obviously, both GHI and its JV partners want to sell the properties for a high price. In 2020, GHI decided to pull in its horns and not sell any properties in a weak and uncertain real estate market. That weak cycle was followed by a very strong upcycle where GHI sold properties at large premiums and had large gains. But real estate doesn't sell on demand. If you want to discount it a lot, you can probably sell fast. If you want to get a fair price, you have to wait for buyers to be in the market. The real estate markets have been slow for a variety of reasons, not the least of which is that high interest rates make buying real estate relatively less attractive.

GHI will realize large chunks of cash when these properties sell. At the minimum, they will get their capital and the preferred return.

GHI's Success With The JV Strategy

If we go back in time to 2020, this is what GHI's JV equity on their 10-K looked like: Source

They reported net equity of $106.8 million across 12 properties. In 2021, they sold three of those properties for over $55 million: Source

Then in 2022, they sold another three properties for nearly $70 million:

In other words, in just two years, they sold only 6 out of the 12 properties for $125 million. They were carrying all of them for $106 million.

GHI could have just dumped its properties in 2020. Maybe they would have gotten $100 million from Brookfield (BN) or Blackstone (BX) and then invested that into MRBs, and then they wouldn't have needed to cut the distribution in 2020.

Instead, management remained patient and recognized that even though these properties weren't easily sold right now, they could wait just a little bit and realize large gains – and they did.

This time isn't different. GHI's current JV equity is at $146 million. They could probably sell it. It's a preferred position in multi-family development; a company like BX would probably be thrilled to buy a preferred interest in already built multi-family properties that are already operating and being managed for a discount. They pay $100 million for a position that will likely monetize for +$200 million in 2-3 years, which is exactly the kind of flip that Private Equity companies love.

Why GHI Is Exiting The JV Strategy

GHI has decided to leave the JV strategy. In this last earnings call, they did a better job of clearly outlining why. Here is CEO Kenneth Rogozinski:

"First, by their nature, our tax-exempt mortgage revenue bond investments earned stable returns based on the net interest spread between the bond interest rate and our related debt financing rate. As a result, we expect increasingly stable earnings as compared to the uneven returns on joint venture equity investments due to that income being recognized primarily upon property sales."

Clearly, the public markets don't like the inherent lumpiness of cash flow from the JV strategy. Maybe GHI would have been better just paying a small fixed distribution and distributing specials with property sales, or implementing an explicit variable policy. The market has punished GHI for not having a flat and stable distribution. Having a flat and stable distribution that relies on property sales is simply impossible. Management believes GHI will trade at a higher valuation if its distribution is flat and stable.

"Second, in recent years, the majority of income allocated to our unitholders has been taxable because of the taxable income from joint venture equity investment sales. As we allocate more capital to mortgage revenue bond investments, we expect that the proportion of income allocated to our unitholders that is tax exempt for federal income tax purposes, will increase in the long term."

One of the appeals of GHI is that a large chunk of their distribution is tax-exempt. The sales of their JV properties isn't. So, by transitioning away from that strategy, GHI will have the vast majority of its distribution tax-exempt.

"Third, we are investing capital in a proven asset class that is core to our operations and also leverages the strong relationships and knowledge base of Greystone's other lending platforms."

The MRB business is where GHI has its roots. The JV strategy started as a side business and gradually grew, primarily through its success in producing large returns with a portion reinvested.

Roughly Half Of Equity Is In JVs Today

Today, roughly half of GHI's total equity is invested in the JV strategy. In part because management reinvested proceeds and in part because MRB prices are correlated with municipal bond prices and have declined. The MRB portfolio is marked-to-market, and the JV investments are carried at cost-basis minus impairments.

So as we sit here today, only half of GHI's equity is producing cash flow since no properties have been sold. And management acknowledges this is going to take time:

"We and the Board of managers acknowledge that it will take some time to cycle our capital out of our market rate JV equity investments and into taxes and mortgage revenue bond investments. We currently report minimal earnings related to our JV equity investments during the holding period."

We would rather absorb a dividend reduction than for management to be in a hurry to sell properties for the sake of freeing up capital. This is a voluntary transition, and there is no reason to hurry.

Unprecedented Discount To Book Value

As it stands, GHI is trading at a 55% discount to book value – which, one caller noted that, excluding the JV properties, book value was around $5.57, which means the market is essentially assigning zero value to the JVs.

"Kenneth Rogozinski Chief Executive Officer

But John, it's Ken, to your point about the math, you went through there. I think that's a fair way to say that we would have to have a total loss of all of our invested capital on all of our joint venture market rate multifamily transactions in order for the book value to reduce to that level.

And as Jesse just said, we do have to go through that quarterly evaluation for impairment process on all those investments."

Is it annoying that GHI can't be selling properties quickly? Yes. It would be wonderful if the Fed would cut interest rates, the U.S. economy would pick up, and investors started buying apartments like it's 2021. But, real estate is in an ebb, and multi-family real estate has been slowing down due to oversupply from properties where construction started in 2021-2023. With construction slowing down, it is only a matter of time before the oversupply swings back to undersupply. High construction costs and high interest rates have discouraged new construction starts.

When GHI slashed its distribution in 2020, we wrote:

"ATAX's book value held up extremely well in Q1, and we expect that their book value will remain relatively resilient. This provides us 30% upside to book value, and even more upside if ATAX goes back to trading at a premium. We expect that upside will be realized in time, likely within a year, regardless of what ATAX decides to do with their dividend."

One year after we wrote that, it was trading near book value again. GHI is actually trading at a materially larger discount to book value today. This is a management team that will pass along earnings as they are made. Like in 2020, they've decided not to push forward with selling at poor prices. They are willing to be patient and let the market recover.

That is absolutely the right decision and the decision I would make if I were a sole proprietor running the company. It is better to wait for the real estate market to provide good prices, and sell those properties for a $50-$60 million gain than to just exit at a discount and get something like $100 million and book a $50 million loss for the sake of pleasing the market for a quarter.

Conclusion

We view GHI as an incredible buying opportunity. We don't believe the JV equity is worth $0. It's very likely that those properties are worth materially more than the amount spent to build them if the seller is willing to be patient and wait out the real estate cycle.

The worst thing management could do is rush to sell those properties. Selling at a discount would lead to a reduction in book value that couldn't be easily recovered. It would mean lower distributions in the future when management does reinvest the proceeds into MRBs.

The good news is that management isn't showing signs of rushing. They are making the best long-term decision, rather than the decision that pleases the market next quarter.

Which is why GHI is a long-term holding for me and why the more the market sells, the more we buy.

data.title: Coeur Mining's Correction Looks Overdone Now
data.description:
Still Bullish On CDE

After rising by over 350% since early May 2025, Coeur Mining, Inc. (CDE) stock started to correct in March 2026, following the general market weakness and also the weakening underlying commodity markets like gold and silver.

I called CDE a "Buy" in early December, and the stock first gained over 60%, but then lost most of its gains, now trading only 3-4% higher - that's better than the S&P 500's (SP500) performance, but still, the ugly price action looks terrifying for some investors.

I don't view the change in narrative around CDE as something structural. In other words, there's little fundamental reason for turning bearish on CDE right now. I agree that commodities have been down sharply over the past month, and the firm missed on the top line in its last reporting quarter, but Coeur's restructuring should bring in a lot of value for its shareholders anyway, and the stock's correction as of late has only added to the buying opportunity. That's why I remain bullish on the stock on this heavy dip, foreseeing higher stock prices by the end of this year.

My Updated Reasoning On CDE's Prospects

Coeur reported for its Q4 results on February 19, 2026, showing $674.7 million in revenues (+120% YoY) and ~$0.35 in adjusted EPS (+218% YoY). On the bottom line, CDE beat by 5%, according to Seeking Alpha, but on the top line, we saw a slight miss of ~2%. I don't think it was the main reason for the stock's recently ugly price action because when the Q4 data came out, CDE closed over 6% higher on the day. Plus, we see that the sell-side analysts covering the stock have turned more bullish on the upcoming Q1 print.

I see CDE's business prospects naturally and fundamentally improving, which is going to hold true for the next quarters as well. First, the firm tripled its EBITDA to $1.025 billion in Q4, having generated $666 million in full-year free cash flow (in FY20024, we saw a net cash burn of ~$9 million). In Q4 alone, FCF surged by 66% QoQ, and it looked like a truly massive gain. Basically, in Q4, we saw 2 key operational gains - first in Rochester (Nevada) and the second one in Las Chispas (Mexico).

Specifically in Rochester, CDE achieved the record output, placing 9.3 million tonnes of ore on the leach pads - it brought in ~$78 million in FCF for the quarter. In Las Chispas, which was initially acquired via the SilverCrest transaction in early 2025, Coeur saw a massive $286 million in free cash flow addition in just 10.5 months of operations.

So, these assets helped CDE get to its current net cash position, having $554 million in cash and equivalents as of the end of Q4, which is pretty solid considering the debt reduction of ~42% (to just $342 million) in a single year (FY2025 vs. FY2024). Based on YCharts data, CDE's debt-to-equity is currently at its 10-year minimal level, which is great to see:

Among the most recent corporate events, we see that Coeur closed its monumental all-stock acquisition of New Gold Inc., issuing ~393 million new shares to absorb the Canadian miner - the post-acquisition consolidated company consists of 7 key assets that are placed in the mining-friendly jurisdictions of the United States, Canada, and Mexico, with minimal country risks whatsoever. It matters because for miners that have this kind of corporate structure and asset composition in their portfolios, the market tends to grant a higher valuation multiple. We'll come back to that a bit later, but it's a very important aspect if we exclude the operational gains that CDE is about to get from its new assets in British Columbia and Ontario.

What's coming next for CDE? Well, I see a massive step-up in production because, leaning on the 9 months of New Gold assets' operations, the firm's management issued a very bullish guidance. They expect to produce 680-815,000 ounces of gold, about 18.7-21.9 million ounces of silver, and 50-65 million pounds of copper, so the resulting revenue mix should shift to ~65% gold, ~30% silver, and ~5% copper. The PP&E reserves have grown meaningfully following the New Gold acquisition, promising stable production level for years to come, in my opinion.

The updated guidance suggests that the adjusted EBITDA for the full year should be at ~$3 billion, and the FCF should arrive at ~$2 billion. As CDE has already dedicated significant drilling to the K-Zone, where the management noted 47.6 million tonnes os resources (715,000 ounces of gold and 606 million pounds of copper), I expect that CDE won't dip much in terms of its forward EBITDA and FCF generation capacity even if gold/silver/copper prices correct slightly further from here. Plus, they keep optimizing Rochester's operations, and the management noted that they recently added 2 years to the reserve-based mine life at Rainy River. The production capacity is set to grow, in my understanding, and that's why I don't see any setback in EPS generation starting in 2028, as it's currently projected by the consensus estimates (unless we see a sharp correction in gold or silver prices two years from now - it's hard to predict, and I'd definitely not price it in today).

The shareholder returns are going to be boosted by the buyback program that the management enhanced lately - they authorized a $750 million share repurchase program as a countermeasure to the issuance of shares for New Gold. With the FCF and EBITDA generation that's coming in 2026, I believe we'll see another buyback program authorization, and it's likely to lead to another round of bullish revisions to the bottom line:

Assuming only 5% premium on the FY2027 EPS, and recalling the fact that CDE should trade at a premium to most of its peers because its main asset base is located in the US, Canada, and Mexico, I think the stock should trade at a FY2027 P/E ratio of 10-12x. Right now, the forward ratio is at ~7.22x, so the undervaluation is already visible.

If CDE reprices to the P/E range that I've just mentioned above, and we do see the 5% premium on top of the FY2027 EPS consensus, my price target arrives at ~$32.46/share:

$2.81 * 1.05 * 11x = ~$32.46/share

Source: Oakoff's calculations

That's a massive 90% upside potential to the current stock price. Okay, even if CDE doesn't reach that 5% premium, and it trades at 9x FY2027 earnings, we'd still see a PT of $25.29/share, with a major upside potential of ~47%. Even Wall Street analysts see an upside of over 61%:

Risks

The integration of the assets that Coeur has recently acquired is a very complicated task that has plenty of execution risks that investors should be aware of. If the consolidated firm fails to deliver the synergies that I'm expecting, and the margins fall throughout 2026-2027, my above assumptions that I used for valuation calculations will be misleading.

Commodity prices might fall abruptly and cause a lot of trouble for all miners, especially those whose stock prices have increased dramatically over the past few months - like CDE's.

Also, as I pointed out in my previous article on CDE, if the management returns to speculative growth instead of disciplined capital returns, the stock's re‑rating could stall, and my bullish findings on the upside may become pointless.

Your Takeaway

The risks seem to be high, I agree, but the potential rewards seem to be higher given the latest asset base expansion in the key areas, and taking into account the updated guidance that seems to be incorrectly priced in by the market after the stock's March 2026 correction. I think the sell-off is largely overdone now, and my valuation model says there's an upside of 47-90% in the next 12-24 months. Based on all this, I believe I should leave my Buy rating on the CDE stock untouched for now.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: John Hancock Freedom 529 2041-2044 Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • U.S. stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, although upward momentum slowed in the final quarter of the year.
  • The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on AI, and the Fed's interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December.
  • Healthcare, a laggard for much of 2025, was the top-performing sector in the S&P 500 Index during Q4.

Market review and outlook

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500 Index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed's) interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large caps had only a slight advantage over small caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented healthcare sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI. All other groups lagged the index. Notably, IT, which represented 35% of the S&P 500 Index in Q4, rose about 1%.

International developed-market stocks advanced 4.89%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index. The discrepancy between value and growth stocks was more pronounced in this index, again in favor of value over growth. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 4.72% rise. South Korea (+27%) and Taiwan (+10%) were two strong components with sizable representation in this index, bolstered by the strength in technology stocks in those markets. On the other hand, China finished the quarter with a roughly -7% return, giving back some of its gains since the April bottom.

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 1.1% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Quarterly performance was fueled by a combination of elevated starting yields and price appreciation, as bond prices rose amid falling interest rates and investor demand, supported by stable fundamentals.

The U.S. equity bull market is entering its fourth year, having put up double-digit gains in each of the past three, leaving investors wondering whether it can keep the streak alive. While multiyear stretches of positive equity returns are common, double-digit returns four years in row are few and far between. The naysayers point to elevated valuations, narrowness of the market, uncertainty around Fed policy amid upcoming leadership change, a softening labor market, and U.S. mid-term election risk. Yet, history has shown that bull markets don't die of old age alone, and while valuations are elevated, they're below prior cycle extremes. Bulls can also point to still-robust earnings expectations, a broadening of AI beneficiaries, fiscal support, increased capital spending, deregulation, rising mergers-and-acquisitions activity, and easing trade tensions. With the risks reasonably balanced and momentum still intact, the path of least resistance for the equity market may continue to be higher for now. The question is, can it reach double digits for the four-peat?

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a single-digit gain for the quarter and slightly trailed its blended benchmark, comprising a 70% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index and a 30% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net). Disciplined Value International Fund (John Hancock) was a noteworthy detractor for the quarter. The fund's lower allocation to value stocks and higher allocation to growth versus the MSCI EAFE Value Index was a headwind in a quarter where international value equities strongly outperformed growth names. Disciplined Value Fund (John Hancock) also detracted. Stock selection in healthcare, communication services, industrials, and consumer discretionary proved disappointing for the quarter. Conversely, International Growth Fund (John Hancock) contributed on a relative basis, largely due to security selection in the financials sector. U.S. Growth Fund (John Hancock) also contributed, aided by stock selection in IT and industrials. A tactical overweight to international value equities further contributed, given the relatively strong performance of that group.

This commentary reflects the views of the portfolio managers named and is subject to change as market and other conditions warrant. No forecasts are guaranteed. This commentary is provided for informational purposes only and is not an endorsement of any security, mutual fund, sector, or index. The subadvisor, the advisor (John Hancock Investment Management LLC), and their affiliates, employees, and clients, may hold or trade the securities mentioned in this commentary. Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of a loss.

What you should know before investing

Investing involves risks, including the potential loss of principal. There is no guarantee that a portfolio's investment strategy will be successful or that education expenses will be met. Even if you contribute the maximum amount, there is no assurance that the money in your account will be sufficient to cover all the education expenses your beneficiary may incur or that the rate of return on your investment will match or exceed the rate at which education expenses may rise. The impact of inflation on education expenses is uncertain and could exceed the return on investments in your account. Please see the Plan Disclosure Document for additional risks.

data.title: 3 Monthly Dividend REITs For Passive Income
data.description:
Rental property investments are very popular for one main reason: they can generate significant monthly income.

Tenants pay you rent every month for the right to occupy your property, and it generally requires relatively little work from the landlord.

As such, rentals can be a great investment if you are looking to generate monthly income in retirement or are simply seeking to achieve financial freedom.

But REITs (VNQ) are even better.

Lots of them also pay their dividends on a monthly basis, but unlike rentals, which still require some effort from their owners, REITs are 100% passive investments. You have a professional management team that handles everything for you in a highly cost-efficient way, as they enjoy significant economies of scale. The typical management cost is only about 50 basis points of assets each year, far lower than most other private real estate investments:

Then, on top of that, REIT investors also enjoy the benefits of diversification, liquidity, limited liability, and leverage, without having to sign any of the mortgages themselves.

Finally, because REITs now exist in 40+ countries, investors have 1,000+ different options to evaluate. As such, there are always some opportunities available somewhere, if you know where to look.

In what follows, I am going to highlight 3 of my favorite monthly dividend-paying REITs to buy in today's market, ranked from the lowest to the highest yielder.

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA / BSRTF)

BSR REIT is an apartment REIT that focuses almost exclusively on the rapidly growing Texas Triangle of Dallas, Austin, and Houston.

The REIT has strategically decided to focus on these specific markets as they are expected to keep attracting lots of businesses and wealthy individuals, who are deciding to relocate there to reduce their tax burden and other costs, while also improving their quality of life. This then leads to lots of new job creations, attracting even more people, resulting in rapid population growth:

This trend has existed for a long time already, and yet, apartment rents remain very reasonable and present a lot more long-term upside potential.

BSR owns primarily new-built Class A/B+ garden style apartment communities with attractive amenities in growing sub-markets, and yet, its average rent is today just $1,500. This represents just about 20% of their tenants' household income, compared to the more typical 35% in gateway markets.

Therefore, the long-term prospects are very attractive for these assets. The big private equity players like Blackstone (BX) and Brookfield (BAM) agree as they have been aggressively accumulating assets in these key markets.

But the rent growth is not without its bumps.

The pandemic led to some bumpiness with rents initially surging double-digits from 2020 to 2022, but this strong growth, coupled with near-0% interest rates, then caught the attention of property developers, who started building all over the place. This led to a historic wave of new supply from 2022 to 2025, which cooled down fundamentals, with occupancy rates and rents declining slightly in recent years.

As a result of this, BSR REIT is today hated by the market. Most investors are short-term oriented, and all they see is the oversupply putting pressure on rents, and because of this, investors have left the REIT, and it now trades at a steep 40% discount to its net asset value.

But what the market is missing is that this oversupply is only a temporary issue. Real estate markets are cyclical, and times of oversupply are commonly followed by times of undersupply as developers get hurt, scale back projects, and the demand eventually catches up to the supply.

This is exactly what is happening right now. The new construction starts and deliveries are collapsing and should return to historic lows in the second half of this year:

This bodes well for 2027, as BSR REIT and other apartment REITs are expecting a strong acceleration in rent growth.

I predict that this will change the narrative, improve the sentiment for the stock, and lead to a strong recovery in its share price.

Right now, the REIT trades as if it were facing poor long-term growth prospects, but that perception could change rapidly.

Today, you still get the chance to buy an equity stake in these assets at essentially 60 cents on the dollar, and while you wait for the upside, the REIT pays you a monthly 5.3% dividend yield, typically classified as "return of capital" to increase tax-efficiency, and the REIT is able buying back shares to create more value for patient shareholders.

That's far more attractive than paying full market price for a private rental property in my opinion.

Canadian Net REIT (NET.UN:CA / CNNRF)

Now we move up to a 6% dividend yield, paid monthly.

Canadian Net REIT is the only net lease REIT that operates in Canada. It owns primarily service-oriented properties such as convenience stores, pharmacies, gas stations, and quick-service restaurants, typically leased to credit tenants on a long-term basis with steady annual rent escalations, resulting in highly consistent and predictable recession-resistant income:

As such, it is similar to Realty Income (O) in the US, but it has a few key advantages that have historically made it far more rewarding to its shareholders.

First, it is far smaller in size, which means that each new acquisition truly moves the needle for them. It is far easier to grow your FFO per share at a rapid pace by acquiring properties when you own $300 million worth of assets than $50 billion.

Second, the REIT is dealing with far less competition for deals in Canada, as it is the only local publicly-listed net lease REIT operating there. This results in higher cap rates than in the US for comparable assets.

Third, the REIT has access to cheaper debt as interest rates are lower in Canada, despite the properties trading at higher cap rates. This results in higher investment spreads and faster growth, especially since Candian Net REIT uses a bit more leverage than its US peers.

Here are the results:

The REIT has managed to grow its FFO per share about 3x faster than its larger US peers that deal with greater competition and higher interest rates:

This has also allowed it to grow its dividend at a 2x faster rate than its US peers, all while also lowering its payout ratio to a historic low of just 50%, which is far lower than the average of its US peers.

The low payout ratio allows the REIT to retain significant cash flow to keep reinvesting in growth, even when it cannot raise equity due to its low valuation. It makes its future less dependent on public capital markets.

Yet, the REIT is today discounted, trading at just 10x its cash flow, and offering a near 6% dividend yield, paid monthly. Even with just 4% annual long-term growth, which is far short of its historic average, the REIT could generate double-digit total returns.

Gladstone Land (LANDP)

Now we move closer to an 8% dividend yield, again paid monthly.

Gladstone Land is one of just two REITs that specialize in farmland investments. It owns a well-diversified portfolio, primarily of permanent crops like fruits and vegetables, resulting in defensive and inflation-proof income:

However, you should note that the REIT is quite heavily leveraged with a 60% LTV when including its preferred equity.

Moreover, its common dividend is poorly covered at the moment and at high risk of being cut in the near-term.

For these reasons, we are not interested in the common equity of the company. Rather, we are interested in its preferred equity, which is heavily discounted as well, trading at a 20% discount to par, despite being far safer as it is higher on the capital stack.

While the REIT is heavily leveraged, its debt maturities are fairly long and well-staggered:

Moreover, a common dividend cut would actually benefit the preferred equity as it would allow the REIT to retain more cash flow to deleverage and/or reinvest in growth, which would leave even more cushion for the preferred equity.

For now, the preferred dividend is very well covered, and I have a hard time seeing the cash flows drop materially, given that we are talking about farmland investments.

It is a riskier pick than BSR or NET due to the leverage, but the high 8% yield and potential 25% upside to par value compensate us well for it.

Investor Takeaway

The REIT market is today presenting historic opportunities for high-yield-seeking investors.

Their valuations have dropped to a decade low, with many REITs trading at steep discounts relative to the fair value of their properties.

This results in higher yields, but also upside potential and margin of safety. We are taking advantage of this at High Yield Landlord as we continue to build our monthly-paying high-yielding real estate portfolio.

data.title: Goldman Sachs International Equity Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The MSCI EAFE Index returned 4.86% in Q4 2025 and has returned 31.22% YTD (in USD).

Eurozone stocks posted strong gains in the fourth quarter of 2025, with market indices reaching their highest levels in years and outperforming U.S. stocks. Value sectors such as Financials, Healthcare and Utilities lead the index while growth-oriented stocks were challenged as investors grew wary of elevated valuations. Inflationary pressures eased, prompting the European Central Bank (ECB) to maintain its policy rate in December. Additionally, the ECB revised its estimate for third-quarter 2025 eurozone GDP growth upward from 0.2% to 0.3%, signaling a modest pickup in economic activity.

In the United Kingdom, stock markets closed out 2025 near their highest levels in recent years. The FTSE All-Share Index advanced by 24% for the year (local currency). Global demand, higher commodity prices, and a slightly depreciated pound were catalysts for the Financials, Materials, and Defense sectors which led the index.

The Japanese equity market, as tracked by the TOPIX index, delivered a robust performance in the fourth quarter, achieving a total return of approximately 8.8% for the quarter and an annual gain of 25% (local currency terms) to close the year at a record high. This strong showing was primarily driven by resilient corporate earnings and a weaker yen which benefited exporters. The top-performing sectors during the quarter included electronics, banking, and construction, while the bottom-performing sectors included utilities and real estate investment trusts (REITs). Stretched valuations led to volatility among AI-related names toward the end of the quarter as well. From a macroeconomic perspective, Japan experienced greater political stability, proactive fiscal stimulus, and a shift to higher interest rates in late 2025, fueling economic optimism and strong equity market performance.

Please refer to the Fund's prospectus for the most recent expenses.

Investment Commentary

Portfolio Attribution

The Goldman Sachs International Equity Income Fund (GSIKX) outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Index, by +306 basis points (bps), net of fees, in 4Q 2025.

At the region level, stock selection in Europe and Japan were the greatest contributors to relative performance. On the other hand, our overweight to North America was the only detractor. At the sector level, stock selection in Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary were the greatest contributors to performance whereas Consumer Staples was the only detractor.

NatWest (NWG), a British banking, insurance and financial services provider, was the largest contributor to performance. NatWest posted another quarter of strong earnings with a 13% year-over-year increase in income. Management raised guidance, giving investors further confidence that the bank's high 2025 Return on Tangible Equity is sustainable. After a period of declining margins due to competitive pressures in the UK banking industry, we believe margins have troughed and expect stabilization and recovery. We like NatWest's capital generation and capital discipline with a 40% dividend payout ratio.

Rio Tinto (RIO), a leader in the global mining industry based in the United Kingdom, was another contributor to performance. Its strong performance was driven by higher commodity prices and renewed optimism regarding its growth pipeline, especially in iron ore, copper, and lithium. Investment sentiment also improved as the company outlined plans for production growth, productivity gains, and disciplined capital spending. Rio Tinto stands out among diversified miners due to its strong operational recovery and disciplined cost control, maintaining robust returns despite softer iron ore markets in recent years. We believe the company is positioned to benefit from the increased steel demand, specifically in China, that will result from the global energy transition. Moreover, Rio Tinto's increased capital investments in other key metals will allow it to benefit from revenue stream diversification over the long term.

Swiss Re (SSREY), a leading global reinsurer with a sizeable commercial lines business, was the top detractor to performance. Swiss Re's stock experienced downward pressure after the company issued a warning in November about a potential full-year profit shortfall in its Life and Health division. Additionally, shares declined further in December when Swiss Re announced a 2026 group net income target of $4.5 billion, which fell short of analysts' expectations of $4.81 billion. As Commercial and Reinsurance rates have remained under pressure and have served as a headwind for Swiss Re, we decided to sell out of the name.

3i Group (TGOPY), a British private equity and infrastructure company, was another detractor. The company was negatively impacted by weakening sales growth at discount retailer Action, which weighed on investor sentiment as Action comprises nearly one-third of 3i Group's total revenue. The company also warned that Action's sales growth may fall below its full-year like-for-like sales guidance of 6.1%. We remain confident in the name as it offers access to quality mid-market unquoted companies with strong growth prospects. Action stands out for its potential due to its store expansion and early entry into Eastern Europe. Current economic pressures across Europe make discount retail appealing, and Action's focused product range delivers strong value and consistent results.

Investment Commentary

Portfolio Review

We initiated a position in Roche Holding (2.07%) , a Swiss multinational healthcare company that develops and manufactures pharmaceutical and diagnostic products. We see long-term potential in the company's early Alzheimer's drug trontinimab and products within Roche's obesity pipeline.

We initiated a position in Allianz (ALIZY) (2.06%) , a German financial services company that offers integrated insurance and asset management solutions globally. We are constructive on the name due to its diversified business offerings, global scale, and execution capabilities. Specifically, Allianz stands out among sector peers by achieving 8.5% organic growth driven by its Retail, Life, and Asset Management focus, along with notable improvements in loss and expense ratios. With fewer headwinds than its competitors and multiple avenues for market share gains and M&A, we are confident Allianz will continue to outperform its targets.

We exited our position in Swiss Re (0.00%) , a leading global reinsurer with a sizeable commercial lines business. With Commercial and Reinsurance rates under pressure, the stock continued to be challenged by these headwinds.

Outlook

Markets have experienced plenty of volatility in 2025 due to economic, political, and geopolitical uncertainty and may continue to do so through 2026. New realities that took shape last year – including elevated geopolitical risk and megatrends rapidly transforming industries – continue to create a complex environment of evolving opportunities and risks in global markets. We believe there may be opportunities to broaden equity exposures beyond the largest US names and into international markets. For instance, we are constructive on the structural economic drivers in Europe and corporate governance reforms in Japanese equity markets.

As active investors, we have continued to build meaningful positions in high quality resilient businesses and complement them with select cyclical exposure to companies that are likely to extend their leadership. We select companies because of our confidence in their ability to grow, and prosper relative to their competitors, over the economic cycle. We are fundamental investors and will remain focused on the long-term rather than trying to time the ups and downs of short-term market gyrations.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Mistral Loads Up On Nvidia
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Mistral raises $830M debt to buy chips for AI data center: report. (00:14) DeepSeek experiences longest outage since launch, going offline for seven hours. (01:14) "Stupidly Cheap": Bill Ackman sees 10X potential in Fannie and Freddie amid Middle East conflict. (01:54)

This is an abridged transcript.

Mistral AI has raised $830M in new ​debt to buy 13,800 Nvidia (NVDA) chips for a ‌data center near Paris.

The debt raising was financed by a consortium of seven banks. According to Reuters, the ​data center is expected to become operational in ​the second quarter of 2026, the report added.

Mistral AI is a French startup backed by ASML (ASML).

Mistral chose the site for its first data center ‌in ⁠February 2025. Last month, the company unveiled plans for a second facility in Sweden and noted that it would seek to secure 200 megawatts of capacity across Europe by the end of ​2027.

Mistral did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

In September 2025, ASML (ASML) agreed to invest €1.3B in Mistral AI, giving the chip equipment maker an 11% stake in the French startup. This also marked one of Europe's largest AI deals.

Mistral has also seen investment from Microsoft (MSFT).

DeepSeek’s chatbot experienced a significant outage lasting over seven hours overnight in China.

This prompted the AI company to roll out multiple updates to fix the problem.

Users began reporting faults on Sunday evening, according to Downdetector.

The startup’s own status page acknowledged an initial issue at 9:35 p.m. before marking the incident resolved two hours later.

Subsequent updates on Monday showed DeepSeek addressing another case of performance issues that took until 10:33 a.m. to be fixed.

DeepSeek had more than 355 million users as of February.

Billionaire investor Bill Ackman took to X, describing the Middle East conflict as one-sided and bullish for equities, and urged investors to “buy quality” as some of the world’s leading companies trade at unusually low prices.

"Ignore the MSM. One of the most one-sided wars in history that will end well for the U.S. and the world," Ackman said in his post. "And we have the potential for a large peace dividend. One of the best times in a long time to buy quality. Ignore the bears," he added.

Ackman, who is returning to the IPO market with a combined offering for his hedge fund manager and a new closed-end fund, further noted that mortgage giants Fannie and Freddie "are stupidly cheap. Asymmetry at its best. They could be a 10X, and it could happen soon.”

Famed "Big Short" investor Michael Burry responded directly to Ackman's post, saying, "Cannot emphasize enough how rare this is in this market."

Both FNMA and FMCC shares have fallen more than 60% over the past six months, reaching their 52-week lows earlier this month.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

BofA strategist sees weak stocks and bonds, strong dollar into Q2

Goldman: War-driven sell-off improves setup for U.S. stocks ahead of key earnings

Suspicious trades around Trump policy moves raise insider concerns

Catalyst watch:

  • The four-day World Vaccine Congress will begin. Merck (MRK), Tonix Pharmaceuticals (TNXP), and Valneva (VALN) are some of the notable companies presenting.

  • It is the 55th anniversary of the founding of Starbucks (SBUX).

data.title: ClearBridge Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: U.S. equities delivered resilient but volatile performance in the fourth quarter. While major indexes remained close to all-time highs, market conditions shifted meaningfully beneath the surface. The S&P 500 Index returned 2.7% during the quarter, while the benchmark Russell Midcap Growth Index declined 3.7%.
  • Contributors: Holdings in Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX), Broadcom (AVGO) and Freeport-McMoRan (FCX). Stock selection in information technology ((IT)) and communication services, an overweight to materials.
  • Detractors: Holdings in e.l.f. Beauty (ELF), Doximity (DOCS) and Robinhood Markets (HOOD). Overweights to IT and communication services, stock selection in consumer staples.
  • Outlook: Markets remain resilient, but volatility has increased as momentum-driven areas cooled and stock-level dispersion widened, reinforcing the importance of disciplined stock selection and a balanced approach to portfolio construction.

Performance Review

  • The Fund outperformed the benchmark gross of fees, driven by a combination of balanced portfolio construction and strong stock selection. Early in the quarter, momentum tied to AI and technology supported results, while the portfolio's exposure to steadier compounders and defensive growth names helped protect performance as volatility increased.
  • Shopify (SHOP), an e-commerce platform that enables merchants to operate and scale digital storefronts, benefited from continued adoption by larger enterprises and improving monetization across its ecosystem.
  • While communication services was the greatest detractor within the benchmark, our differentiated positioning supported relative outperformance. For example, a number of portfolio holdings in sports and live media, such as Madison Square Garden Sports (MSGS), Madison Square Garden Entertainment (MSGE) and TKO Group (TKO), were positive contributors in the quarter.
  • Following a period of strong performance, shares of e.l.f. Beauty, a cosmetics company focused on affordable, digitally native products, declined as sales growth was hurt by management's decision to stop shipments to retailers that were slow to pass through tariff-related price increases. That said, we are encouraged that consumption trends for the brand remain healthy and management is already seeing shipment growth recover in the current quarter.
  • Within health care, top individual contributor Vertex Pharmaceuticals benefited from growing optimism around its kidney disease pipeline and continued to demonstrate the value of idiosyncratic, innovation-driven growth.
  • Within industrials, for instance, we rotated our exposure in the fourth quarter from Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL), a leading less-than-truckload (LTL) carrier, into XPO (XPO), the fourth-largest LTL provider in North America. We remain mindful of the still-challenging macro environment for freight and see greater idiosyncratic growth potential at XPO. Under new leadership, the company is improving service levels, pricing discipline and margins, leaving more room for outperformance should macro malaise persist.

Outlook

  • Our outlook remains consistent with what we articulated earlier in the year — and importantly, what played out in the fourth quarter. Markets remain resilient, but volatility has increased as momentum-driven areas cooled and stock-level dispersion widened, reinforcing the importance of disciplined stock selection and a balanced approach to portfolio construction.
  • AI continues to represent a powerful long-term opportunity, though early beneficiaries such as semiconductors and infrastructure have already seen significant gains. We are focused on making sure we have not only the right exposure within the AI complex but also other offensive bets in the portfolio to position the Strategy well should market leadership broaden further. At the same time, we continue to emphasize balance, owning companies not only with offensive growth potential but also defensive characteristics built on strong free cash flow, clean balance sheets and proven management teams, which should help to mitigate the portfolio's risk should the environment weaken.
  • We remain opportunistic — taking advantage of pockets when stocks are oversold and trimming when enthusiasm runs ahead of fundamentals — while staying committed to a bottom-up, high active share, and long-term oriented approach. We believe this discipline positions the Strategy well to navigate continued volatility, identify durable winners beyond broad market trends, and drive long-term performance for clients.
