data.title: PDX: Trades At A 10% Discount And Aligned To Capture AI Power Demand
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered the PIMCO Dynamic Income Strategy Fund (PDX), I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation at the time. The fund also had a strong history of producing solid returns, and I expected the fund to provide a supplemental distribution at year-end. Since my last coverage, the fund did pay out its largest special dividend to date, and despite the share price declining by more than 10%, the inclusion of distributions helped PDX offset the poor performance of the market indices. Since my last coverage, the fund has released updated reporting, and the macro environment looks a lot riskier than it did a few quarters ago. Therefore, I wanted to provide some updated thoughts on the fund's outlook for 2026.

When I previously covered the fund, PDX traded at a discount to NAV of 8%. Following the decline in share price, PDX now trades at a discount to NAV of 10.13%. This new valuation is more attractive than the time of my last coverage, but less attractive than the fund's average discount to NAV of 12.57% over the last five years. However, I anticipate that PDX will eventually trade at a premium to NAV valuation once market conditions improve and investors fully recognize the fund's strength and long-term potential.

The latest annual report indicates some weakness in the fund's ability to provide attractive net realized gains, but I think this was just a reflection of the market, rather than challenges within its portfolio. Looking forward, a lot of the potential of the fund is locked behind the requirement of lower interest rates. Assuming that interest rates continue to trend lower over the next twelve months, this can serve as a strong growth catalyst, as well as position the fund to capture the rising power demand. However, a scenario where interest rate cuts are delayed can continue to impede NAV growth. So let's start by taking a look at the fund's updated structure.

Fund Strategy & Outlook

According to the latest fund overview, PDX has total managed assets of $1.32B that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. As the fund's name implies, PDX utilizes a blended strategy, and its portfolio will be dynamic based on market conditions. Management aims to provide exposure to the many different credit markets by utilizing different securities and making adjustments to its strategy based on the cyclical economic outlook. The fund is still relatively new, with an inception dating back to 2019, and it offers a reasonable management fee of 1.25%, considering the active management of its portfolio.

The main focus is ensuring that its portfolio can produce enough income to generate cash flow that can support the high distributions paid to investors. When looking at the blended portfolio, we can see that PDX does have a 33.56% allocation to equities. However, the majority of these equities sat within midstream and pipeline companies. This is tied to the fund's strategy of allocating at least 25% of its assets to energy companies. This exposure is primarily followed by an allocation to high-yield credit securities and other income-producing instruments.

I specifically want to highlight the column label as 'DWE%', which presents the duration-weighted exposure. The 'other' portion of its portfolio has a 76.99% duration-weighted exposure, which likely consists of asset-based securities. Generally, a segment labeled as other will contain different types of securitized debt securities. Since I don't see them listed, I assume that this would mostly include different types of mortgage-related positions, which introduces interest rate sensitivity for the fund. So despite this portion of the portfolio only making up 30.78% of total assets, it is likely to have an influence on PDX's share price.

This portion of the portfolio is likely to benefit in a scenario where interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months. Lower interest rates tend to cause a bond's value to rise, while also improving the fund's operating spread. However, interest rates remain near the higher end of their ten-year range, and the Fed has decided to leave rates unchanged during its latest meeting.

On a more positive note, I do like PDX's positioning with its midstream companies. However, the largest position is currently in Venture Global, Inc. (VG), at 11.39% of assets. VG is now up more than 157% YTD related to a recent victory in a legal battle with Repsol, S.A. (REPYY). Repsol initially had a 20-year contract to buy natural gas from VG, but then the Russia-Ukraine war stalled those efforts. While Repsol waited, VG was selling gas to other customers at the elevated market prices and raking in higher profits. Despite this drama, VG also secured another 20-year contract with Korea's Hanwha Aerospace.

Aside from its largest position, I believe that PDX is aligned to capture the growth of the AI data center market. With the rising demand for AI, data centers require a high baseload of power that solar and wind cannot currently service. Therefore, the main beneficiary will be the midstream natural gas companies that PDX has direct exposure to, such as MPLX LP (MPLX), Energy Transfer LP (ET), Enbridge Inc. (ENB), and Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD), just to name a few. After combing through these positions, I was able to find some instances where these businesses are already positioning themselves to service this demand:

  • Energy Transfer: advancing major pipeline expansions and raised its 2026 EBITDA guidance to $17.8B, guided by rising data center demand.
  • TC Energy Corporation (TRP): the management expects more than $10B in near-term opportunities to meet rising natural gas demand, led by data centers.

Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI data center market can increase to $810.6B by 2033, up from the current level of $180.6B. This represents an estimated CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 23.9% over the next few years. Even if this estimate is off by 50%, this would still represent a double-digit growth catalyst that PDX is directly aligned to participate in.

Performance Vulnerabilities

Since a large portion of the fund is allocated to debt securities, the success of PDX ultimately relies on a lower interest rate environment. If the Fed remains hawkish, this can slow earnings growth, cause position values to decline, and increase pessimism within the debt markets. With rising tensions in Iran, an increase in unemployment, and crude oil hovering near the $100 per barrel mark, there's no telling which direction interest rates will go. Furthermore, the fund also utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings.

If this leverage is allocated to securities that are sensitive to higher rates, this can have a negative impact on the underlying earnings. The amount of effective leverage currently amounts to 22.01% of assets, which is equivalent to $307.4M. I do consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage in this higher interest rate environment since it can negatively impact the fund's operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities. Since interest rates are high, a greater portion of the earnings needs to be allocated towards interest expenses as well, which may cause the net investment income to decline.

Looking at the most recent semi-annual report for 2026, we can see how net investment income amounted to $0.71 per share, while net realized losses landed at $2.67 per share. However, the report is dated through the end of December 2025, so this doesn't fully capture the recent upside growth of VG over the last few months, and we must keep that in mind. That means the fund may have a concentrated point of failure if the holding faces any specific unexpected headwinds at some point this year.

Furthermore, the fund's net investment income isn't strong enough to support distributions on its own. As a result, PDX is very reliant on positive net realized gains to bridge the gap in its earnings, which can support NAV growth and distribution coverage. The variability of net realized gains makes it hard to determine how the fund will perform during any given period. For instance, market indices raced to new all-time highs in the second half of 2025, but PDX still experienced a net realized loss of $2.67 per share.

Furthermore, investors need to remember that PDX is essentially selling off all of its gains to produce a high distribution. Therefore, the fund's capacity to provide capital appreciation is minimal. For instance, the fund's share price has only increased by about 8.8% since inception. Additionally, PDX experienced a large drawdown in 2020, likely due to the fact that PDX was faced with the scenario of paying out more than it actually generated. Therefore, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their total return will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions over time.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1334 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.2%. The listed dividend yield may look a bit inflated because of the most recent special dividend that was issued at the end of 2025. This special dividend totaled $2.97 per share, which was implemented so that the fund can meet its 2025 distribution requirements for federal exercise tax purposes. It appears that the fund was able to lock in some large capital gains from its positions and rewarded investors handsomely. The fund did issue a special at the end of 2024 as well, but that was much smaller at $0.37 per share.

If the estimates are correct and the AI data center market continues to rise over the next few years, I anticipate that PDX will be able to continue providing year-end special dividends as long as earnings exceed the base distribution. If we multiply the base distribution of $0.1334 per share by twelve months, we get an estimated annual base distribution of $1.6008 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, the fund has demonstrated that it can easily generate earnings that exceed payouts. This was demonstrated through the 2025 period:

2025's earnings of $3.68 per share / $1.6008 annual payout = ~2.29 years.

Lastly, investors need to consider the risk that all dividends received may not offer any tax efficiency. Since payouts are being supported by the combination of net investment income and net realized gains, distributions may be classified as ordinary income, which has the highest tax consequences for investors. Therefore, a position in PDX would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors who want to limit their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that PDX remains an attractive buy at this time. The fund is directly positioned to benefit from the rising power demand as AI data centers grow and the price of oil remains high. As PDX's positions continue to expand earnings, the fund can generate excess earnings that can lead to more special dividends in the future. Furthermore, PDX will also benefit from an environment of lower interest rates because it can improve the operating spread and lead to higher values for its debt securities. However, the fund is admittedly overweight in its top position and may be vulnerable to a scenario where interest rates remain high.

data.title: OXLCM: An 8.25% Term Preferred IPO From Oxford Lane Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

With this, we continue our series of articles focused on the fixed-income financial products newly listed on the exchange. This time, we'll apply our IPO evaluation methodology to the latest listed security of Oxford Lane Capital Corporation (OXLC)—the 8.25% Term Preferred Series 2031 Due March 2031, Oxford Lane Capital Corp. CAL NT 31 (OXLCM). OXLC has had quite a NAV per share and price drop in recent months, so we'd like to know whether we'd like any exposure to its preferred equity. For this, we'll follow our IPO evaluation routine, starting with an analysis of the financial state of the issuer to assess the potential dangers of investing in its fixed-income products. Next, we'll continue with peer comparisons from similar issuers to assess the investment qualities of OXLCM in a relative value analysis.

The New Issue

The prospectus contains all relevant information about the new preferred term. Here, we will highlight only the most important metrics for the analysis that follows.

OXLCM will pay an 8.25% annual fixed cumulative dividend in $0.1719 monthly payments. The distributions will begin on April 30, 2026, meaning that the first dividend will be irregular in size and will amount to approximately $0.2292 per share. OXLC's preferred distributions are treated as ordinary income. OXLCM is a term preferred stock of Oxford Lane, with a maturity date on March 20, 2031, and callable on and after March 20, 2028. Oxford Lane's gross proceeds from the new security are $60 million for a total of 2.4 million preferred stocks issued with a liquidation preference of $25 each. The stock was not credit-rated at IPO by any of the big rating agencies.

As the issue is just listed on the exchange, its price chart looks like this:

Currently, OXLCM is priced at $24.59, with a yield to maturity of 9.04% calculated by the XIRR function. The tables below show the beginning of the internal rate of return calculation table and how the YTM of the security would change with a price variation around its current price:

In a chart visualization:

Using the same internal rate of return method, the yield to call is calculated to be 9.62%. This is what the stock's YTC curve looks like right now:

Company basics and performance

Oxford Lane Capital Corp. is a well-established, publicly traded closed-end fund with a stated main purpose to maximize its portfolio's risk-adjusted total return. To achieve this, the management team constructs a portfolio that consists predominantly of equity tranches of collateralized loan obligations and, to a much lower scale, of CLO-debt tranches:

A note for the table above: for Oxford Lane, the third fiscal quarter of 2026 ends on December 31, 2025. The CLO equity tranches are entitled to all the surplus interest and principal collections from the underlying portfolio, should such exist. They, however, are also the first-loss position in downturn periods. When effectively the whole portfolio consists of the riskiest tranche, and there are lingering concerns about the private credit, this is how the NAV per share develops:

The combination of the lower and lower mark-to-market evaluation of the portfolio and the long-term policy of Oxford Lane to distribute to the shareholders more than the portfolio generates has led to a drop of 50% in the NAV per share compared to a year ago.

While this should be the major concern of the investors, it seems to me that the 1-for-5 reverse stock split and the two dividend cuts, which are a good step by the management team trying to prevent the NAV erosion, are actually the reasons that led to something that seemed unthinkable until recently—OXLC to be consistently priced at a discount:

The problem for us, as fixed-income investors, is that this already lost premium to NAV was a layer of safety when putting our money into the baby bonds and term preferreds of Oxford Lane Capital and the other similar CEFs. The long-term market desire to price OXLC at a premium gave us a nice opportunity to hedge the credit risk when investing in its fixed-income products. We recently showed that these types of investments accounted for more than 20% of our return in 2025. However, shorting something at a discount does not have the same appeal as shorting something way overpriced on a consistent, illogical premium. This means that now, when OXLC seems more fairly priced, its baby bonds and term preferreds are less appealing for us.

Asset Coverage Ratio

The capital structure of OXLC is shown below. With approximately $1.8 billion in total assets and $0.7 billion in leverage financing, the fund has approximately 39% of effective leverage, or 256% of asset coverage ratio:

OXLCM will increase the preferred equity by $60 million. It all depends on how the proceeds of this offering will be used:

We estimate that the net proceeds to us from this offering will be approximately $57,833,000 (or approximately $66,551,750 if the underwriters fully exercise their overallotment option), after deducting the payment of underwriting discounts and commissions of $1,875,000 (or approximately $2,156,250 if the underwriters fully exercise their overallotment option) and estimated offering expenses of approximately $292,000 payable by us.

We intend to use all of the net proceeds from this offering to redeem a portion of our outstanding Preferred Stock and/or for general working capital purposes.

Source: OXLCM Prospectus.

In the first extreme case, when all the proceeds are used to redeem already outstanding preferred equity, nothing fundamentally changes for the fund. In the second extreme, when all proceeds go for new asset acquisition, then the asset coverage ratio will drop to approximately 244%, leading Oxford Lane closer to the 200% regulatory required asset coverage minimum.

If we fail to maintain an asset coverage ratio of at least 200% (as described in this prospectus supplement), we will redeem a portion of the outstanding 8.25% Series 2031 Term Preferred Shares in an amount at least equal to the lesser of (1) the minimum number of shares of 8.25% Series 2031 Term Preferred Shares necessary to cause us to meet our required asset coverage ratio and (2) the maximum number of 8.25% Series 2031 Term Preferred Shares that we can redeem out of cash legally available for such redemption.

...

In addition, our Articles Supplementary for the Term Preferred Shares prohibit us from declaring a common stock distribution if, at the time of declaration, our asset coverage ratio is not at least 200% after deducting the amount of such distribution.

Source: OXLCM Prospectus

Please keep in mind that the total investment value as of December 31, 2025, was $2.26 billion. If this trend of fast devaluation continues, it will bring the fund even closer to the mandatory 200% ACR requirement and all the above-mentioned consequences of potentially breaching it.

Dividend Coverage

Since April 2026, Oxford Lane will pay its common shareholders the already decreased dividend of $0.20 per share monthly. As of February 28, 2026, the Company had approximately 97.6 million shares of common stock issued and outstanding. We can calculate approximately $19.5 million in expected common shares distributions monthly. The already outstanding preferred shares have approximately $14.1 million in dividends per year, and OXLCM will increase this value by $5 million to a total of approximately $19.1 million. This means that after the 50% cut, the common shares' dividend cushion is still more than adequate, providing 100% coverage of the annual preferred dividends with a month's worth of the OXLC distribution.

Oxford Lane products comparison

Besides OXLCM, Oxford Lane has three more term preferreds and four baby bonds outstanding. Here are their metrics at this moment:

And here are the IPO credit spreads:

The previous fixed-income IPO of Oxford Lane was the baby bond OXLCG a year ago. All else being equal, it is normal for the preferred OXLCM to have a higher risk premium. However, 90 basis points is a bit too much. So, underwriters also reflect the fact that the fund has become a bit riskier issuer compared to a year ago.

Looking at the nominal yields of the securities, how they are priced, and knowing that Oxford Lane can currently issue preferred equity at 8.25% and debt possibly at 7.75%-8.00%, it doesn't look like the company has the initiative to refinance anything but OXLCI. For all the reasons described above, I currently find no appeal in investing in any of the Oxford Lane fixed-income products. However, if I HAD to pick one, it would be OXLCZ. When a company becomes too risky for our liking, the shortest-duration product often provides the best risk/reward ratio. The debt lies higher in the capital structure, so this is also in favor of the baby bond over the preferreds.

Sector Comparison

The charts below contain all baby bonds and term preferreds with maturity dates in the closed-end fund—CLO segment. The stocks are currently priced under or at par, so the comparison will be by the YTM metric:

And the relevant metrics:

OXLCM has the highest YTM in this group. This, however, reflects the problems we pointed out so far. Five years to maturity is way too much for an exposure to a junior fixed-income product of a company that loses 50% of its NAV in 12 months. As I explained, our hedging reaction is gone, so this is another reason not to reach for any of the products. I'd like to see some NAV consolidation and/or OXLC priced at a premium before even considering putting some money into the fund's fixed income.

Mandatory Redemption for Asset Coverage

If we fail to maintain an asset coverage ratio (as defined below) of at least 200% as of the close of business on any Business Day on which asset coverage is required to be calculated, and such failure is not cured by the close of business on the date that is 30 calendar days following such Business Day (referred to in this prospectus supplement as an Asset Coverage Cure Date), then we are required to redeem, within 90 calendar days of the Asset Coverage Cure Date, shares of Preferred Stock equal to the lesser of (1) the minimum number of shares of Preferred Stock that will result in our having an asset coverage ratio of at least 200% and (2) the maximum number of shares of Preferred Stock that can be redeemed out of funds legally available for such redemption. Also, at our sole discretion, we may redeem a number of shares of Preferred Stock (including shares of Preferred Stock required to be redeemed) that will result in our having an asset coverage ratio of up to and including 285%. The Preferred Stock to be redeemed may include, at our sole option, any number or proportion of the 8.25% Series 2031 Term Preferred Shares and other series of Preferred Stock.

Source: OXLCM Prospectus.

Conclusion

This is an informational article about the new term preferred stock OXLCM. With these articles, we want to pay attention to all new preferred stocks and baby bonds, and they are a good guide to what to expect from your income portfolio.

Oxford Lane is currently not in a good place. The two dividend cuts in the past twelve months could very well limit the NAV erosion, and although a good move, they created another problem for the management. Cutting the distribution of the common stock in combination with a reverse stock split made the “income seekers” discard their positions at large, pricing OXLC consistently for months at a discount. This closed another channel for adding to the NAV—issuing new shares at a premium. For us, the discount means no more hedging reaction at a premium. This, in combination with the still dropping NAV per share, makes the investment in OXLC's fixed income unappealing at the moment.
